summary: Stancil argues that software businesses inevitably need to charge for products, and the open‑source “build in public, then monetize” path predictably leads to backlash even though the outcome is obvious. He uses the Fivetran–Tobiko deal and OpenAI’s Statsig acquisition to show how monetization and data access incentives shape behavior, especially in AI and data tooling. The pattern repeats in data infra: niche open‑source tools drift toward BI and LLM‑adjacent products because that’s where the money is.
money_quotes: Open core is a business model for the monetization of commercially produced open-source software; of course it will eventually cost money!

If you build an open source data orchestration tool in 2025, not only will you eventually want to charge money for it; you will also realize that your tool understands business context, that business context is what makes LLMs better at answering questions, and that people seem to pay a lot of money for charts and chatbots.
Nobody is upset at Costco for charging people to shop there because they give away free samples!
But if you’re a big AI company, there’s a third kind of data that might also be valuable: How do people use your competitors’ products?
But eventually—in the best case, if things go well—two things will inevitably happen.
key_insight: The incentives in software and data businesses make “open, community-first” narratives converge on monetization, and in AI the race for data turns acquisitions and telemetry into competitive weapons.
